entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X+1): Entropy Convexity and Ricci Curvature (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X+1): Entropy Convexity and Ricci Curvature (in progress)

entry.content:
This blog post is dedicated to a specific topic that unites entropy, displacement convexity, and Ricci curvature. In my personal opinion, this is one of the deepest and aesthetically appealing results of modern optimal transport theory. I quickly alluded to it in the 4th blog post in this series, but here I will expand from there a little more.

In summary, what I will elaborate is where optimal transport becomes shockingly close to Riemannian geometry. The key idea is due to Felix Otto: the space of probability densities with the 2-Wasserstein metric behaves like an infinite-dimensional Riemannian manifold, and the entropy Hessian in this manifold contains the Ricci tensor of the base manifold.

In case we get confused, in this blog I will specifically add subscripts to distinguish differentiation on \(t\) vs on \(x\).

1] The “manifold” of probability densities

Let

\[(M,g)\]

be a smooth \(d\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold with Riemannian metric \(g\).

Let \(dx\) denote the Riemannian volume measure on \(M\).

We consider the space

\[\mathcal P_2(M) = \left\{ ho(x) \ge 0 : \int_M ho(x)\,dx = 1, \quad \int_M |x|^2 ho(x)\,dx < \infty ight\}.\]

Each element of \(\mathcal P_2(M)\) is a probability density function

\[ ho : M ightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}.\]

We treat (\mathcal P_2(M)) as an infinite-dimensional manifold.

2] Curves of probability densities

Consider a time-dependent probability density

\[ ho_t(x), \quad t \in [0,1],\; x \in M.\]

Thus we have a map (probability flow):

\[ ho : [0,1] imes M ightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}, \quad (t,x) \mapsto ho_t(x).\]

Assume mass is conserved over time:

\[\int_M ho_t(x)\,dx = 1 \quad orall t.\]

Mass conservation implies the continuity equation

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) + abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) v_t(x) ight) = 0.\]

Here

  • \(v_t(x) \in T_xM\) is a velocity vector field
  • \[v : [0,1] imes M o TM\]

3] Tangent vectors in probability space

At a fixed density \( ho_t(x)\), the time derivative

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = \partial_t ho_t(x)\]

acts as a tangent vector in the space of probability measures.

From the continuity equation, we know that

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) v_t(x) ight).\]

Optimal transport theory shows that along Wasserstein geodesics, the velocity field is always a gradient field (we covered this in this previous blog post YY):

\[v_t(x) = abla \phi_t(x)\]

for some scalar potential

\[\phi_t : M o \mathbb{R}.\]

Thus tangent vectors take the form

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla \phi_t(x) ight).\]

Therefore

  • tangent vectors acquires a corresponding scalar potentials \(\phi_t(x)\)

4] Otto’s Riemannian metric

Felix Otto introduced a Riemannian metric on \(\mathcal P_2(M)\).

Suppose we have two tangent vectors:

\[\dot{ ho}^{(1)}(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho(x) abla \phi_1(x) ight)\]

and

\[\dot{ ho}^{(2)}(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho(x) abla \phi_2(x) ight).\]

The Riemannian inner product is defined as

\[\langle \dot{ ho}^{(1)}, \dot{ ho}^{(2)} angle_{ ho} = \int_M g_x ig( abla \phi_1(x), abla \phi_2(x) ig) ho(x)\,dx.\]

Here

  • \(g_x(\cdot,\cdot)\) is the Riemannian metric at \(x\).

How to interpret this? Notice that the squared norm of a tangent vector becomes

\[\| \dot{ ho} \|_ ho^2 = \int_M | abla \phi(x) |_g^2 ho(x)\,dx.\]

This equals the kinetic energy of moving mass.

Indeed, the Benamou–Brenier formula states

\[W_2^2( ho_0, ho_1) = \inf_{ ho_t,v_t} \int_0^1 \int_M |v_t(x)|^2 ho_t(x) \,dx\,dt.\]

5] Wasserstein geodesic equations

Under Otto’s metric, geodesics satisfy the continuity equation:

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) + abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla \phi_t(x) ight) = 0\]

By the Hamilton–Jacobi equation

\[\partial_t \phi_t(x) + rac12 | abla \phi_t(x) |^2 = 0.\]

Solving these equations produces the optimal transport interpolation (optimal transport flow) between

\[ ho_0(x) \quad ext{and} \quad ho_1(x).\]

Just as a sanity check, we should now be comfortable for connecting the static and dynamic view of optimal transport, and easily write out the optimal flow \( ho_t(x)\) as the law of the straight flow once we know the optimal map:

\[ ho_t(x) = ext{Law}((1-t)x + t\Phi^{opt}(x))\]

or, in other words

\[ ho_t = ((1-t) + t\Phi^{opt})_{\#}\]

6] Entropy Functional and Variations

Define the entropy functional as (note whether to take negative sign is just a choice; you could also call it negative entropy if you are familiar with the negative sign in front of the integral)

\[\mathrm{H}( ho) = \int_M ho(x)\log ho(x) \,dx.\]

This is a functional

\[\mathrm{H} : \mathcal P_2(M) ightarrow \mathbb{R}.\]

Then let’s compute the first variation:

\[ rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}(x) = 1+\log ho(x).\]

The Wasserstein gradient flow equation for entropy then becomes

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) = abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}( ho_t(x)) ight).\]

Substitute

\[ rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}(x)= 1+\log ho_t(x).\]

Then

\[ abla (1+\log ho_t(x))= rac{ abla ho_t(x)}{ ho_t(x)}.\]

Thus

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) = abla \cdot \left( abla ho_t(x) ight).\]

Therefore

\[\partial_t ho_t(x)= \Delta_x ho_t(x).\]

Very surprisingly:

The heat equation is the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

This result is known as the Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (JKO) scheme.

7] Second Derivative of Entropy along a Geodesic

Let

\[\{ ho_t(x)\}_{t \, \in \, [0, 1]}\]

be a Wasserstein geodesic generated by the potential

\[\phi_t(x).\]

Compute

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t).\]

After a long calculation using the continuity equation and the Bochner identity, we would obtain

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t) = \int_M \Big( | abla^2 \phi_t(x)|^2 + \mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla \phi_t(x), abla \phi_t(x) ) \Big) ho_t(x) \,dx.\]

Here

  • ( abla^2\phi_t(x) ) is the Hessian of ( \phi_t )
  • ( \mathrm{Ric}_x(\cdot,\cdot) ) is the Ricci curvature tensor at (x).

How to interpret this formula then? Well, notice that the Hessian splits into two terms:

  • Positive Hessian term $$ abla^2\phi_t(x) ^2 \ge 0$$
  • Curvature term \(\mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla\phi_t(x), abla\phi_t(x))\), which depends on the geometry of the manifold.

8] Convexity from Ricci Curvature

Suppose the manifold satisfies

\[\mathrm{Ric}_x(v,v) \ge K|v|^2 \quad ext{for all } v\in T_xM.\]

Then

\[\mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla\phi_t(x), abla\phi_t(x) ) \ge K | abla\phi_t(x) |^2.\]

Thus

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t) \ge K \int_M | abla\phi_t(x) |^2 ho_t(x) \,dx.\]

But along a Wasserstein geodesic,

\[\int_M | abla\phi_t(x) |^2 ho_t(x) \,dx = W_2^2( ho_0, ho_1)\]

Therefore

\[\mathrm{H}( ho_t) ext{ is } K ext{-convex along Wasserstein geodesics}.\]

9] Geometric Meaning

This shows that

\[ ext{Hessian}_{W_2} \mathrm{H} \sim \mathrm{Ric}.\]

So the Ricci curvature of the manifold controls the convexity of entropy in Wasserstein space. This insight leads to the Lott–Sturm–Villani theory, which defines curvature bounds using optimal transport.

Or put it differently, the Ricci curvature ≥ \(K\) if and only if

The entropy functional is (K)-convex along Wasserstein geodesics in the space of probability measures.

10] Lott–Sturm–Villani Theory

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X): Entropy and Fisher Information (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X): Entropy and Fisher Information (in progress)

entry.content:
1] Entropy

1.1] Entropy Definitions

In this sectio, I mean to clarify the relationships between entropy, negative entropy, and their convexity properties under different interpolations of probability distributions.

1.1.1] Discrete Shannon Entropy

For a discrete distribution:

\[H(p) = - \sum_i p_i \log p_i\]

This is the original Shannon entropy.

1.1.2] Differential Entropy (Continuous Case)

For a continuous density \(p(x)\):

\[H(p) = -\int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

This is called differential entropy.

Important properties:

  • It depends on the coordinate system
  • It can be negative
  • It is not invariant under reparameterization

Note that this is not the continuous analogue of the discrete entropy (Shannon entropy).

1.1.3] Boltzmann / Gibbs Entropy

In statistical physics:

\[S = k_B \log W\]

where \(W\) is the number of microstates.

In distribution form (Gibbs entropy):

\[S = -k_B \int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

Thus, up to the constant \(k_B\), this is the same functional as differential entropy.

1.1.4] Negative Entropy

Negative entropy is defined as

\[F(p) = \int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

So

  • Entropy is concave
  • Negative entropy is convex

1.1.5] Relative Entropy

Relative entropy is also frequently known as and referred to by the name of Kullback Leibler (KL) Divergence:

\[D_{KL}(p|q) = \int p(x)\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}dx\]

This should be interpreted as the continuos counterpart of the discrete Shannon entropy. They are both scale invariant. I will elaborate this below.

1.2] Mixture Interpolation of Distributions

Given two distributions \(p\) and \(q\), their mixture interpolation is

\[\rho_t = (1-t)p + tq, \quad t \in [0,1].\]

Interpretation:

  • With probability \(1-t\), sample from \(p\)
  • With probability \(t\), sample from \(q\)

This is a straight line in the linear space of densities.

1.3] Convexity Under Mixtures

1.3.1] Negative Entropy is Mixture Convex

Remember the definition of the negative entropy:

\[F(\rho) = \int \rho(x)\log \rho(x)\,dx\]

For mixture interpolation

\[\rho_t = (1-t)\rho_0 + t\rho_1\]

we have

\[F(\rho_t) \le (1-t)F(\rho_0) + tF(\rho_1).\]

Therefore

Negative entropy is convex under mixtures.

The reason is that \(\phi(x)=x\log x\) is a convex function.

1.3.2] Entropy is Mixture Concave

Differential entropy is

\[H(\rho) = -\int \rho\log\rho.\]

Since it is the negative of a convex functional, then

\[H(\rho_t) \ge (1-t)H(\rho_0) + tH(\rho_1).\]

Thus Differential entropy is concave under mixtures.

1.4] Why Differential Entropy Depends on Coordinates

Now we have a general feel about different kinds of entropy and its convexity properties, you might have a natural question as to why the differential entropy is not a continuous counterpart of the discrete Shannon entropy. Afterall, it seems to be just taking limits. Also, what does it mean to say that differential entropy is not invariant under change of variables. Let’s investigate into this a little bit more.

Let

\[y = g(x).\]

The density transforms according to the Jacobian:

\[p_Y(y) = p_X(x)\left|\frac{dx}{dy}\right|.\]

Computing the entropy after transformation gives (this is not hard; consider as a little exercise for yourself)

\[H(Y) = H(X) + \mathbb{E}[\log |g'(X)|].\]

Thus differential entropy changes under reparameterization.

For example, let

\[X \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1).\]

Then, perhaps a little surprisingly,

\[H(X)=0.\]

Define \(Y=2X\), then \(Y\sim\text{Uniform}(0,2)\) and thus

\[H(Y)=\log 2\]

This demonstrates that simply changing the scale changes differential entropy.

Why does this happen? Well, differential (continuous) entropy involves densities:

\[p(x)=\frac{dP}{dx}.\]

Since the base measure \(dx\) depends on the coordinate system, entropy does too.

In other words, differential entropy is really entropy relative to the Lebesgue measure.

1.5] Coordinate-Invariant Quantities

The good news is, quantities involving density ratios remain invariant. For example: KL divergence

\[D_{KL}(p||q) = \int p(x)\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}dx.\]

Under coordinate change, Jacobians cancel:

\[\frac{p_Y(y)}{q_Y(y)} = \frac{p_X(x)}{q_X(x)}.\]

Thus, KL divergence is coordinate invariant.

1.6] Mixture Geometry vs Transport Geometry

Mixture interpolation does not move mass.

Example:

  • \(p\): Gaussian centered at \(-5\)
  • \(q\): Gaussian centered at \(5\)

Mixture interpolation produces two bumps, because the distributions coexist, but physical transport should produce one bump moving across space.

I also discussed this difference in the previous blog XX.

In optimal transport, particles move. If \(T(x)\) is the optimal transport map, then

\[x_t = (1-t)x + tT(x).\]

The density evolves via

\[\rho_t = ((1-t)\mathrm{Id}+tT)_\# \rho_0.\]

This path is the Wasserstein geodesic.

1.7] Displacement Convexity

The subjects of the following two sections have been discussed in the previous blog and I will expand them in the next post. For now, as a review and also to reinforce our interpreation, please allow me to state the findings again:

McCann (1997) proved that the functional (negative entropy)

\[U(\rho) = \int \rho\log\rho\]

is convex along Wasserstein geodesics:

\[U(\rho_t) \le (1-t)U(\rho_0) + tU(\rho_1).\]

Thus, negative entropy is displacement convex.

Since entropy is the negative of this functional,

\[H(\rho) = -U(\rho),\]

we obtain that entropy is displacement concave.

In summary, differnetial entropy is both mixture concave and displacement concave, and negative entropy is convex in both concepts.

1.8] Why Displacement Convexity Matters

Mixture interpolation corresponds to randomly choosing distributions, where optimal transport corresponds to moving mass in space.

This might sound trivial, but many physical and probabilistic dynamics follow transport geometry.

For example, the Fokker–Planck equation

\[\partial_t \rho = \nabla\cdot(\rho\nabla V)+\Delta\rho\]

Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (1998) showed:

This PDE is the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

This connects optimal transport, diffusion equations, statistical physics, and Riemannian geometry into a unified framework.

2] Fisher Information

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(4): Optimal Transport Flow: the Monge Problem (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(4): Optimal Transport Flow: the Monge Problem (in progress)

entry.content:
1] Optimal Transport Flow Problem Formulation

  • Dynamic Eulerian
  • Dynamic Lagragian
  • Static (Monge Problem)

2] Equivalence of Static and Dynamic Formulations

  • Dynamic <= Static

  • Static <= Dynamic

For Jensen’s inequality to reach equality, the integrad within the expectation has to be time-invariant, which means indicates that \(\Phi_t\) corresponds to a line.

Consequently,

\[\Phi_t(x) = (1-t)x + t\Phi^{opt}(x)\]

associates the optimal flow (dynamical view) with the optimal map (static view).

3] Convexity Issues

  • Claim: the dynamical problem is convex and constraint is linear (infinite-dimensional)

  • However, for the static problem, the objective function is convex, but the constraint is not linear.

4] Dynamic Problem Solution

4.1] Proof

5] Displacement Convexity and Ricci Curvature

This section summarizes what I think is one of the deepest discoveries and constructions in modern optimal transport theory.

\(t ightarrow H[ ho_t]\) is convex.

Implications for convexity

  • Ricci flow to define curvature

(I will have another blog to elaborate on this amazing result)

5.1] Ordinary Convexity in Probability Space

If we view probability measures as a linear space, the natural interpolation between two measures \(\mu\) and \( u\) is the mixture interpolation:

\[ ho_t = (1-t)\mu + t u, \quad t \in [0,1].\]

A functional \(F( ho_t)\) is convex in the usual sense if

\[F ig((1-t)\mu + t u ig) \le (1-t)F(\mu) + tF( u).\]

This is sometimes called mixture convexity. For example, negative entropy is convex under mixtures.

Specifically, recall that the negative entropy is

\[F( ho) = \int ho(x) \log ho(x)\, dx .\]

(so the usual notion of entropy is \(H( ho) = -F( ho)\)). Then we have

\[F((1-t)p + t q) \le (1-t)F(p) + tF(q).\]

Substituting \(F( ho)=\int ho\log ho\),

\[\int ho_t \log ho_t \le (1-t)\int p\log p + t\int q\log q .\]

This is very intuitive, as entropy measures uncertainty, and mixing two distributions increases uncertainty, so entropy goes up:

\[H((1-t)p + tq) \ge (1-t)H(p) + tH(q).\]

Or, we could interpret this algebraically, since

\[\phi(x) = x \log x\]

is convex for \(x>0\).

Since

\[F( ho) = \int \phi( ho(x)) dx,\]

and integrals preserve convexity, $F$ becomes a convex functional.

Thus:

  • Entropy is (mixture) concave
  • Negative entropy is (mixture) convex

This property is fundamental because:

  • It makes KL divergence a Bregman divergence of negative entropy.
  • It ensures variational problems involving entropy are convex.
  • It contrasts with optimal transport geometry, where entropy behaves differently (leading to displacement convexity).

There’s also a very deep connection between

negative entropy convexity \( ightarrow\) KL divergence convexity \( ightarrow\) exponential families \( ightarrow\) mirror descent

which is one of the most beautiful structures in information geometry. Later story.

5.2] Wasserstein Geometry: a Different Notion of Straight Line

Given the great properties above, however, many geometric functionals are not convex under mixture interpolation.

But hold on, you might ask, if mixture convexity holds for negative entropy, why bother studying other kinds of convexity?

This is an excellent and deep question. The short answer is: Mixture convexity lives in the wrong geometry for many physical and dynamical problems, whereas optimal transport reveals the correct geometry of probability space.

Importantly, mixture interpolation is not physical transport. Say we have

\[ ho_t = (1-t)p + tq\]

this literally means flip a coin with heads up with probability \(t\) and down with \((1-t)\). If it’s Head, then sample from \(q\) and sample from \(q\) otherwise. Since we just randomly choose distribution \(p\) or \(q\), it does not move mass. In extreme case, if \(p = \delta_{-5}, q = \delta_{5}\), then mixture interpolation gives us just two bumps, because both distributions coexist.

However, if we kind of expect the interpolation to be smearing/smoothing/averaging them together in some sense, which actually corresponds transport mass and just one bump moving from \(5\) to \(-5\).

This is what Wasserstein geodesics produce.

In optimal transport, probability measures are not treated as a linear space but as a Riemannian-like metric space (Otto’s theory, will elaborate in the following blogs)

\[(\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d), W_2)\]

where $W_2$ is the 2-Wasserstein distance.

In this geometry, the natural notion of a straight line is a Wasserstein geodesic.

Suppose the optimal transport map from \(\mu\) to \( u\) is

\[T(x).\]

Then the geodesic interpolation is

\[ ho_t = ig((1-t)I + tT ig)_{\#}\mu\]

This describes particles moving along straight lines from \(x\) to \(T(x)\).

From the above we shall see that

  • mixture interpolation = blending distributions
  • optimal transport interpolation = moving mass

5.3] Displacement Convexity

A functional \(F( ho)\) is called displacement convex if along the Wasserstein geodesic \( ho_t\)

\[F( ho_t) \le (1-t)F(\mu) + tF( u).\]

5.4] Entropy is Displacement Convex

Consider the differential entropy

\[H( ho)= \int ho(x)\log ho(x)\,dx.\]

(notice that here we’re not using the negative sign (you could add if you want, then just switch convex to concave, not a problem at all)) A fundamental theorem by Robert J. McCann (1997) shows:

The entropy functional is convex along Wasserstein geodesics.

So indeed

\[t \mapsto H[ ho_t]\]

is convex when \( ho_t\) is the optimal transport interpolation. This is called McCann displacement convexity.

This property implies:

  • uniqueness of gradient flows
  • stability of diffusion
  • functional inequalities
  • curvature bounds of spaces

Consequently, entropy behaves nicely in both convexities, but the displacement convexity describes real dynamics.

5.5] Why This Happens (Intuition)

The key reason is that optimal transport preserves the structure of mass movement.

Under optimal transport interpolation:

  • the density evolves according to a continuity equation
  • the Jacobian of the transport map controls density change.

Specifically,

\[ ho_t(x) = rac{ ho_0(y)} {\det ig((1-t)I + tDT(y) ig)}.\]

This determinant interacts with the $\log ho$ term in entropy in a way that produces convexity.

This phenomenon is deeply related to:

  • the Brunn–Minkowski inequality
  • convexity of the log-determinant function

6] Many PDEs Follow Displacement Geometry

Displacement convexity is extremely important because it implies gradient flows in Wasserstein space. Indeed, apart from entropy, many other important equations evolve along Wasserstein geometry, not mixture geometry. For example,

  • Fokker–Planck equation:
\[\partial_t ho = abla \cdot ( ho abla V) + \Delta ho\]

Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (1998) showed:

This PDE is gradient descent of entropy in Wasserstein space.

Specifically,

\[\partial_t ho = abla \cdot ( ho abla \log ho) = \Delta ho\]

So entropy is not just concave along mixtures. It behaves as a convex energy along OT geodesics, and thus heat equation can be interpreted as the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

7] Why This was a Big Discovery

Before optimal transport, probability space was treated as a linear space. Optimal transport revealed it actually has a Riemannian-like geometry. In that geometry:

  • geodesics = mass transport
  • entropy = convex energy
  • diffusion = gradient flow

This unified PDE, probability, geometry, and statistical physics.

8] Entropic Optimal Transport Flow

9]
entry.summary: Static and Dynamic Formulation of Optimal Transport Flow, Duality, Monge Problem

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(3): A Tale of Two Perspectives (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(3): A Tale of Two Perspectives (in progress)

entry.content:
1] Eulerian View

2] Lagragian View

3] An Important Lemma

3.1]

Why doest \(\Phi_t\) being a diffeomorphism lead to the fact that \(\det( abla \Phi_t) > 0\)?

Well, the key point is that being a diffeomorphism forces the Jacobian determinant to never vanish, but the positivity comes from continuity in time together with the initial condition. Let’s go step by step.

In the Lagrangian view of optimal transport we have a flow map

\[\partial_t \Phi_t(x) = V_t(\Phi_t(x)), \qquad \Phi_0(x) = x.\]

Here

  • \(\Phi_t : \mathbb{R}^d o \mathbb{R}^d\) is the flow map
  • \(V_t\) is a smooth velocity field.

So \(\Phi_t\) is generated by an ODE flow.

Then, if \(\Phi_t\) is a diffeomorphism, then

  • \(\Phi_t\) is smooth
  • \(\Phi_t^{-1}\) exists and is smooth.

By the inverse function theorem, this implies

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) eq 0\]

for every \(x\). Otherwise the map would locally collapse dimension and would not be invertible. So diffeomorphism naturally leads to Jacobian determinant never vanishes.

Then Why is the determinant is positive? Now consider the initial condition \(\Phi_0(x) = x\), so

\[ abla \Phi_0(x) = I.\]

Thus

\[\det( abla \Phi_0(x)) = 1.\]

An important fact: The determinant evolves continuously in time because \(\Phi_t\) is smooth in \(t\)! If the determinant were ever to become negative, it would have to cross zero at some time. But

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) = 0\]

would violate the diffeomorphism property. Therefore the determinant cannot change sign. Since it starts positive,

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) > 0\]

for all \(t\).

3.2] Geometric interpretation

\(\det( abla \Phi_t)\) measures local volume distortion.

  • positive determinant → orientation preserved
  • negative determinant → orientation flipped

A smooth flow starting from the identity cannot suddenly flip orientation without collapsing volume first. But collapsing volume would break invertibility. So the flow preserves orientation.

3.3] Another way to see it (Jacobian ODE)

Define

\[J_t(x) = \det( abla \Phi_t(x)).\]

Then one can derive

\[ rac{d}{dt} J_t(x) = ( abla \cdot V_t)(\Phi_t(x))\, J_t(x).\]

Solving:

\[J_t(x) = \exp\!\left( \int_0^t ( abla\cdot V_s)(\Phi_s(x))\,ds ight).\]

Since the exponential is always positive,

\[J_t(x) > 0.\]

3.3] Final intuition

A diffeomorphic flow generated by a smooth velocity field:

  • starts from the identity
  • continuously deforms space
  • never tears or folds space

Therefore

\[\det( abla \Phi_t) > 0\]

because the map cannot cross zero Jacobian without losing invertibility.

4] Equivalence and Proof

5] Velocity Fields on Example Probability Flows

5.1] Ex.1 Linear Interpolation

Note: \(\Phi\) being a diffeomorphism does not necessitate that \(\Phi_t\) is also a diffeomorphism (might not be invertible)!

5.2] Overdamped Langevin Flow

5.3] Mixture Flow

5.4] Convolution FLow

\( ext{Law} ( ilde{\Phi}_t(X_0, X_1)) eq ext{Law}(\Phi_t(X))\). The left hand side is a fake flow (crossings could happen).

5.5] Heat Flow

Heat flow is also a convolution flow.

Two derivations of vector fields inform each other.

Association with the Tweedie’s formula, score, and Diffusion Model.

5.6] Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Flow

Just Langevin dynamics with Gaussian target; or convolution flow similar to the heat flow.

5.7] Poisson Flow
entry.summary: Eulerian and Lagrangian Interpretation, Vector Fields Optimal Transport

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(2): Transport Maps and Couplings (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(2): Transport Maps and Couplings (in progress)

entry.content:
1] Transport Map

1.1] Definition

1.2] Measure Preservation

1.3] Pushforward Sampling

1.4] Change of Variables Formula

1.5] Co-area Fomula

1.6] Transport Maps in 1 Dimension

Similaity to transition maps in manifolds between coordinate domains?

Optimal Transport maps are in some sense generalizations of this 1D transport map based on CDF’s.

1.7] Transport Maps in High Dimensions (Knothe-Rosenblatt map)

2] Coupling

2.1] Definition

From joint distribution to coupling.

Note taht this set is alwasy nonempty, because we could always take the product measure (\(X\) and \(Y\) are independent, if you may)

2.2] Examples

2.2.1] Deterministic vs. Product Coupling

Almost the opposite of product coupling and they are almost never the same, even in your setting where

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu .\]

Let’s go through the structure carefully, first of all deterministic coupling induced by the map. Let

\[X \sim \mu, \qquad Y = \Phi(X).\]

Then the joint distribution of \((X,Y)\) is

\[\gamma = ( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\]

Explicitly, for measurable sets \(A,B\):

\[\gamma(A imes B) = \mu ig(\{x\in A : \phi(x)\in B\} ig).\]

This coupling has marginals

  • first marginal: \(\mu\)
  • second marginal: \(\phi_{\#}\mu = u\)

so it is indeed a coupling between \(\mu\) and \( u\).

The key property for the above joint law is that the conditional distribution satisfies

\[Y|X=x \sim \delta_{\phi(x)}(y).\]

So \(Y\) is completely determined by \(X\). This is why it is called a deterministic coupling.

On the other hand, there always exist the produc coupling:

\[\gamma_{ ext{prod}} = \mu \otimes u.\]

For measurable sets:

\[\gamma_{ ext{prod}}(A imes B) = \mu(A) u(B).\]

Its conditional distribution is

\[Y|X=x \sim u.\]

So here

  • \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent.

The deterministic coupling and the product coupling are different. The deterministic coupling lives on the graph of \(\phi\):

\[\{(x,\phi(x))\}.\]

whereas the product coupling spreads mass over the entire product space.

Well, this actually has some practical advantages for transport map instead of coupling (transport plan) in terms of parameter storage, and in fact quite often we will be able to find a transport map in optimal transport problem, which is indeed in most cases preferrable. We will come back to this later.

2.2.2] An Example

Let

\[X \sim ext{Uniform}[0,1], \qquad \Phi(x)=x.\]

Then

\[ u = \mu.\]

For the deterministic coupling, the joint law is

\[(X,X)\]

Support: \(\{(x,x)\}\), which is just the diagonal line.

On the other hand, for the product coupling, the joint law stays on

\[(X,Y), \quad X,Y ext{ i.i.d. Uniform}[0,1]\]

where the support is \([0,1]^2\), which is the whole square.

These are clearly different distributions.

2.2.3] When Would They Coincide?

They coincide only in degenerate cases. For example if \( u\) is a Dirac measure

\[ u = \delta_{y_0}\]

and

\[\Phi(x)=y_0.\]

Then

  • deterministic coupling: $(X,y_0)$
  • product coupling: $(X,Y)$ with $Y=y_0$ a.s.

So they match in this specific case, but this is exceptional.

What I want to emphasize is that knowing that

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\]

does not restrict couplings to deterministic ones.

It only tells us one particular coupling exists:

\[( ext{Id},\Phi)_{\#}\mu .\]

But the set of couplings

\[\Pi(\mu, u)\]

is huge — it includes

  • deterministic couplings
  • product coupling
  • infinitely many others.

Later in optimal transport, we will observe that the deterministic coupling

\[( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\]

is called a transport map, but most couplings are transport plans that cannot be written as maps.

In summary, if we indeed have a map \(Y=\Phi(X)\), then we would naturally obtain a deterministic coupling \(\gamma = ( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\)

while in most cases it’s different from product coupling, which always exists between two measures

\[\gamma = \mu\otimes u\]

\(X\) and \(Y\) independent.

They are almost never the same.

3] Clarifications and Differences

It’s not always possible to establish a transport map from \(\mu\) to \( u\).

For example, the Kronecker Delta.

However, apart from this, in general is there a transport map?

If we could then find out a transport map between \(\mu\) to \( u\), then does that mean that there exists a deterministic coupling among others?

4] A Broader Geometric Picture

It’s hard to conceal the deeper truth behind such differences and connections between deterministic vs product coupling. However, readers not familiar with optimal transport (I will cover that in the not too distance future) feel free to skip this and come back later. Specifically, here I would like to clarify how deterministic couplings sit inside the full coupling polytope and why optimal transport often selects them. The following applied to all couplings when $ u = \Phi_{#}\mu$

Suppose

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\]

for some measurable map \(\Phi : X o Y\), or we could just say \(Y = \Phi(X), \qquad X \sim \mu\).

This means

\[\gamma(X, Y) = \mu(X)\delta_{\Phi(X)}(Y)\]

is one possible coupling, or one transport plan between \(\mu\) and \( u\).

However, from the above discussions we should already be intuitive and comfortable with the idea that the space of all couplings between \(\mu\) and \( u\) is much larger. Consequently, understanding this space geometrically helps clarify the role of deterministic transport maps.

4.1] The Space of All Couplings

The set of couplings is

\[\Pi(\mu, u) = \{\gamma \in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y) : (\pi_X)_{\#}\gamma = \mu,\; (\pi_Y)_{\#}\gamma = u \}.\]

So every coupling

  • spreads mass over the product space \(X imes Y\)
  • while preserving the two marginals.

Geometrically:

  • \(\Pi(\mu, u)\) is a convex set (I’ll let readers to prove this claim)
  • Mixtures of couplings remain couplings.

Indeed, if

\[\gamma_1,\gamma_2 \in \Pi(\mu, u)\]

then

\[\gamma_t = t\gamma_1 + (1-t)\gamma_2, \,\, t \in [0, 1]\]

is also a valid coupling (this is almost obvious).

So $\Pi(\mu, u)$ behaves like a convex polytope (or convex body) in the space of measures.

4.2] Deterministic Couplings = Graphs

The deterministic coupling induced by $\Phi$ is

\[\gamma_{\Phi} = (\mathrm{Id},\Phi)_{\#}\mu.\]

Its support is

\[ ext{supp}(\gamma_{\Phi}) = \{(x,\Phi(x))\}.\]

which is the graph of \(\Phi\).

So geometrically, all mass lies on a lower-dimensional surface, instead of filling the whole product space.

For example (the same as above), if

\[X=Y=[0,1], \quad \Phi(x)=x\]

then

  • deterministic coupling → diagonal line
  • product coupling → full square

4.3] Discrete Case: The Transportation Polytope

The geometry becomes very clear in the finite case.

Suppose

\[\mu = (a_1,\dots,a_n), \qquad u = (b_1,\dots,b_m).\]

A coupling is a matrix

\[\Gamma = (\gamma_{ij})\]

satisfying

\[\sum_j \gamma_{ij} = a_i\] \[\sum_i \gamma_{ij} = b_j\] \[\gamma_{ij} \ge 0\]

This set forms a transportation polytope. Perhaps it’s a little more intuitive to see that geometrically it’s a convex polytope in \(\mathbb{R}^{nm}\).

Consequently, for this problem the vertices of this polytope correspond to deterministic transport plans.

In particular

  • extreme points have at most \(n+m-1\) nonzero entries
  • many correspond to permutation-like assignments.

These are the most structured couplings.

Another perspective is to interpret this as a linear programming optimization problem, which I will elaborate more later when actually go into optimal transport!

4.4] Where the Deterministic Map Lives

When \( u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\), the coupling \(\gamma_\Phi\) lies inside $\Pi(\mu, u)$. More precisely, it sits on the boundary of this convex set. Why?

Because it concentrates mass on the graph constraint

\[y=\Phi(x).\]

This is a very low-entropy configuration. In contrast:

  • product coupling spreads mass everywhere
  • mixtures smear the distribution across the square.

4.5] Why Optimal Transport Selects Deterministic Maps

Optimal transport (Kantorovich problem) solves

\[\min_{\gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u)} \int c(x,y)\, d\gamma(x,y).\]

But notice that this optimization problem is

A linear objective over a convex set

Since

  • \(\Pi(\mu, u)\) is convex
  • It’s linear in \(\gamma\)
  • deterministic plans correspond to extreme points

the optimizer often lands on a deterministic transport map.

4.6] Brenier’s Theorem (quadratic cost)

If

  • \(\mu\) is absolutely continuous
  • the cost is
\[c(x,y)=|x-y|^2\]

then the optimal coupling is

\[\gamma = (\mathrm{Id},T)_{\#}\mu\]

for a map

\[T = abla \psi\]

where $\psi$ is convex. Notice that the Brenier’s Theorem is special not because it exposes that \(T\) exists and is a gradient (which has been known to many even before Brenier for a long time), but that it’s a gradient of a convex function. Conseuqently, the optimal plan collapses onto a deterministic graph.

4.7] Entropy Perspective

Deterministic coupling corresponds to minimum entropy, while product coupling indicates maximum entropy. Later on we will discuss one practical alrogithm (Sinkhorn) for solving the optimal transport optimizaton problem, on a slightly regugalirzed form of this problem called Entropic Optimal Transport, which intentionally pushes solutions away from deterministic vertices toward the interior of the polytope.

4.8] Intuitive Summary

Key ideas:

  • deterministic couplings sit on the boundary/extreme points
  • mixtures fill the interior
  • optimal transport prefers extreme points, which explains why transport maps appear naturally

Even if \( u=\Phi_{\#}\mu\), the deterministic coupling is just one corner of a huge convex set of possible couplings — optimal transport often picks that corner because linear costs prefer extreme points of the coupling polytope.

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(1): Probability Flows (in progress)

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(1): Probability Flows (in progress)

entry.content:
This is the starting blog of a new series. Distinct from the Geometry Mumble series, this collection of blogs will primarily deal with the other side of my interest: the temporal effects along sampling.

0] High Dimensional Sampling

Sampling from a complex, arbitrary, high-dimensional distribution—like trying to find a needle in a haystack where the haystack is the size of the solar system—is one of the most significant hurdles in modern statistics and machine learning. When we say “arbitrary,” we mean the distribution doesn’t follow a nice, predictable shape like a Gaussian (Bell Curve). When we say “high-dimensional,” we mean we are dealing with hundreds, thousands, or even millions of variables simultaneously.

0.1] The Curse of Dimensionality

As the number of dimensions (\(d\)) increases, the “volume” of the space grows exponentially. If we want to maintain the same density of sample points in 100-dimensional space as we have in 2-dimensional space, we would need more samples than there are atoms in the observable universe.

  • Concentration of Measure: In high dimensions, most of the “mass” of a distribution lives in a very thin “shell” or manifold far away from the mode (the peak) (check this blogs and this blog).
  • The Search Problem: Most of the space is “empty” (near-zero probability). A random sampler will spend almost all its time exploring regions that contribute nothing to the overall understanding of the distribution.

0.2] The Normalization Constant (The Partition Function)

Most complex distributions are defined by a function \(f(x)\), but to make it a true probability distribution, it must integrate to 1. This requires dividing by a normalization constant \(Z\):

\[P(x) = rac{f(x)}{Z} ext{ where } Z = \int f(x) dx\]

In high dimensions, calculating this integral is computationally intractable. While algorithms like Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) allow us to sample without knowing \(Z\), they face significant efficiency bottlenecks.

(> Note: Modern techniques like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) attempt to solve these issues by using the gradient of the distribution to “glide” along the geometry, much like a puck sliding on a frictionless surface).

0.3] Multimodality and “Islands”

A complex distribution often has multiple “modes” (peaks) separated by vast valleys of low probability.

  • Trapping: A sampler might get stuck in one peak, unaware that a much larger peak exists elsewhere.
  • Mixing Time: The time it takes for a sampler to “forget” its starting position and move between these islands grows exponentially with the height and width of the barriers between them.

0.4] Anisotropy and Local Geometry

In high dimensions, the distribution might be very “stretched” in some directions and “compressed” in others.

  • Step Size Dilemma: If your sampler takes steps that are too large, it overshoots the narrow regions and gets rejected. If the steps are too small, it takes forever to explore the long, stretched dimensions.
  • Correlation: When variables are highly correlated, the “path” the sampler must follow is a narrow, winding ridge in a massive space.

1] Probability Flows

Flows of probability measures underly a variety of techniques and theories, ranging from generative modeling like Diffusion and Flow based models, or more classical domains of math like optimal transport. Many seemingly intuitive formulations carry with themselves deep fundamental explanations. This blog will start from a very basic survey of options of changing, the different probability flows. But before that, to have an intuitive feeling of probability flows embedded in our mind, let’s start with a glimpse of one of our ultimate problems to solve, the continuous Kantarovich transport problem:

\[\inf_{\pi \in \Pi(X, Y)} \int c(x, y)d\pi(x, y)\]

where \(c: \mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is a selected cost function, \(X, Y \in \mathbb{R}^d\) random variables (notice that here this is actually not a rigorous notation for rv) from proability measures \(\mu, u\) respectively, and \(\Pi(X, Y)\) is the space of couplings between \(\mu\) and \( u\).

Let’s say we simplify the above by further requiring that \(\mu = u\) and let’s say that \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent. If this is the case, then what is the best coupling?

Very obviously, the answer is that it will be an identity coupling, basically not to move the probability measures at all. On the other hand, the free option, product coupling, will result in

\[E(|X - Y|^2) = Var(X)\]

which is positive. Consequently, althought we always have methods to move measures from one to the other, among these various selection to find out the “optimal” one is never a trivial question.

For all the blogs in this series, I will observe the convention that \(\mu\) is the source measure, \( u\) is the target measure, and I’ll not repeat this specification unless otherwise it changes.

Our basic question framework goes as the following: Given \(\mu, u\), we want to find out \({ ho}_{t \in [0, 1]}\) such that \( ho_0 = \mu, ho_1 = u\).

1.1] Mixture Flow

1.2] Convolution Flow

1.3] Langevin Flow

1.4] Föllmer Flow

1.5] Optimal Flow

2] Continuity Equation

The Continuity Equation is the mathematical expression of a “no-leak” policy. In fluid dynamics, it represents the conservation of mass; in probability theory and optimal transport, it represents the conservation of total probability mass (ensuring the integral remains $1$ as the distribution evolves; or, the measure is preserved).

2.1] Derivation

Imagine a fixed region of space (a “control volume”) $V$ with a boundary surface $S$. If the amount of “stuff” (mass, probability, or charge) inside $V$ changes over time, it must be because that stuff flowed across the boundary $S$.

We define:

  • \( ho_t(x)\): The density at point $x$ and time $t$.
  • \(\mathbf{v}_t(x)\): The velocity field (or vector field) pushing the density.

Then, the total amount of “stuff” \(M\) inside the volume \(V\) is given by the volume integral:

\[M = \int_V ho_t(x) dV\]

The rate of change of this mass over time is \( rac{dM}{dt}\). According to the conservation principle:

Rate of change inside \(V\) = \(-\)(Net flux passing out through boundary \(S\))

The flux through a tiny element of the surface \(dS\) is \( ho \mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} dS\), where \(\mathbf{n}\) is the outward-pointing unit normal vector. Thus:

\[ rac{d}{dt} \int_V ho_t dV = -\oint_S ( ho \mathbf{v}) \cdot \mathbf{n} dS\]

To turn this into a purely local (differential) equation, we need to convert the surface integral into a volume integral. We use the Divergence Theorem (also known as Gauss’s Theorem):

\[\oint_S ( ho \mathbf{v}) \cdot \mathbf{n} dS = \int_V abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{v}) dV\]

Substituting this back into our conservation equation:

\[\int_V rac{\partial ho_t}{\partial t} dV = -\int_V abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{v}) dV\]

Since this equality must hold true for any arbitrary volume $V$, the integrands themselves must be equal at every point in space. By moving everything to one side, we arrive at the Continuity Equation:

\[ rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{v}) = 0\]

2.2] Interpretation of Terms

  • \( rac{\partial ho}{\partial t}\): The local rate of change of density at a fixed point.

  • \( abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{v})\): The divergence of the flux. If this is positive, it means the flow is “spreading out” from that point, leading to a decrease in local density.

2.3] Connection to Optimal Transport & ML

In Optimal Transport and Generative Modeling (like Flow-matching), we use this to describe how a simple base distribution $ ho_0$ deforms into a complex data distribution $ ho_1$.

Instead of moving “particles” randomly, we solve for a velocity field $\mathbf{v}_t$ that transports the probability mass efficiently. This is the core of the Benamou-Brenier formulation:

\[\min_{ ho, \mathbf{v}} \int_0^1 \int_{\mathbb{R}^d} ho(x, t) \|\mathbf{v}(x, t)\|^2 dx dt\]

subject to

\[ rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{v}) = 0\]

This constraint ensures that as we “morph” the distribution, we aren’t creating or destroying probability—we are simply moving it along the most energy-efficient paths.

I will elaborate more on this later in the following blogs.

3] Deriving Vector Fields

With the continuity equation, we would actually observe that all probability flow examples in section 1 could be refit in this formula.

3.1] Mixture Flow Vector Field

3.2] Convolution Flow Vector Field

Under suitable decay / boundary conditions, the two expressions are equal. Let’s derive it carefully: We have

\[\int -e^{izx}\,\mathrm{div} ig( ho_t(x)v_t(x) ig)\,dx\]

Let

\[F(x) = ho_t(x)v_t(x)\]

so the integral becomes

\[-\int e^{izx}\,\mathrm{div}F(x)\,dx\]

Now, recall integration by parts (here really just the divergence theorem): For smooth functions with sufficient decay at infinity,

\[\int \phi\,\mathrm{div}F = -\int \langle abla \phi, F angle\]

provided the boundary term vanishes.

Here

\[\phi(x) = e^{izx}\]

so

\[ abla \phi(x) = i z\, e^{izx}\]

Thus

\[-\int e^{izx}\,\mathrm{div}F = \int \langle abla e^{izx}, F(x) angle\]

Substitute the gradient:

\[= \int \langle iz\,e^{izx},\, ho_t(x)v_t(x) angle dx\]

Factor out the exponential:

\[= \int e^{izx}\,\langle iz, v_t(x) angle\, ho_t(x)\,dx\] \[\int -e^{izx}\,\mathrm{div}( ho_t(x)v_t(x))\,dx = \int e^{izx}\,\langle iz, v_t(x) angle ho_t(x)\,dx\]

provided

  • \( ho_t(x)v_t(x)\) decays sufficiently fast, or
  • we work on a torus / periodic domain.

This identity is exactly the Fourier transform of the continuity equation

\[\partial_t ho_t + \mathrm{div}( ho_t v_t) = 0\]

Taking the Fourier transform gives

\[\partial_t \hat{ ho}_t(z) = \int e^{izx}\langle iz, v_t(x) angle ho_t(x)\,dx\]

which is the form used when deriving velocity fields for convolution flows / characteristic functions.

4] Fokker-Planck Equation

The above continuity equation is the basis for another widely used stochastic counterpart: the Fokker-Planck equaion(also known as the Kolmogorov Forward Equation). Indeed, to transform the deterministic flow of the continuity equation into the Fokker-Planck equation, we introduce stochasticity (Brownian motion). This changes the movement from a simple “push” to a combination of “pushing” (drift) and “spreading” (diffusion).

4.1] The Stochastic Starting Point

In the continuity equation, we assumed particles follow a deterministic path \(dx = \mathbf{v_t}(x)dt\). To add “noise,” we use a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE):

\[dx_t = \mathbf{f}(x_t, t)dt + \sqrt{2D} dW_t\]

Where:

  • \(\mathbf{f}(x, t)\): The drift coefficient (the deterministic “push”).
  • \(D\): The diffusion coefficient (the intensity of the noise).
  • \(dW_t\): Standard Brownian motion (Wiener process).

4.2] The Flux with Diffusion

In the deterministic case, the flux was simply

\[\mathbf{J}_{drift} = ho \mathbf{v}\]

When we add random walking (diffusion), particles naturally move from regions of high density to low density. According to Fick’s Law, this additional flux is:

\[\mathbf{J}_{diffusion} = -D abla ho\]

The total flux $\mathbf{J}$ is the sum of the drift and the diffusion:

\[\mathbf{J} = ho \mathbf{f} - D abla ho\]

4.3] Deriving the Equation

We plug this total flux \(\mathbf{J}\) back into the general conservation law we derived earlier (\( rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + abla \cdot \mathbf{J} = 0\)):

\[ rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{f} - D abla ho) = 0\]

Distributing the divergence operator, we get the standard form of the Fokker-Planck Equation:

\[ rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} = - abla \cdot ( ho \mathbf{f}) + D \Delta ho\]

Where $\Delta$ (or $ abla^2$) is the Laplacian operator, representing the spreading of the density.

4.4] Key Differences

Feature Continuity Equation Fokker-Planck Equation
Motion Type Deterministic (Flow) Stochastic (Drift + Diffusion)
Entropy Conserved along trajectories Generally increases over time
Steady State Often requires $\mathbf{v}=0$ Reaches a stationary distribution (e.g., Gibbs)

4.5] Application in ML: Score-Based Models

In Diffusion Models, we define a SDE that turns data into noise. To generate new data, we solve the Reverse-Time SDE. The Fokker-Planck equation ensures that the probability density of our generated samples matches the data distribution at every timestep \(t\).

Similar to the continuity equation, I will elaborate more on this later when we discuss diffusion models. s

entry.title: The Dance of Space: Geom/Topo/Dynam Mumble(6): Symmetric Positive Definite Matrix (in progress)

link.title: The Dance of Space: Geom/Topo/Dynam Mumble(6): Symmetric Positive Definite Matrix (in progress)

entry.content:
We’ve talked about the collection of positive definite matrices on several occasions already, each time focusing on different facets. On the post on Lie group/algebra, I explained that it is not a Lie group but a smooth manifold, and then in the later post, we saw that by introducing Riemannian metric we could turn

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \{ A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \mid A^\top = A,\; A > 0 \}.\]

into a Riemannian manifold, upon which we could calculate distance and geodesics. Specifically, we spent sometime going over the computation of affine invariant distance:

\[d(A,B) = \left\| \log\left(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}\right) \right\|_F = \left( \sum_{i=1}^n (\log \lambda_i)^2 \right)^{1/2}.\]

where \(\lambda_i\) are eigenvalues of \(A^{-1}B\).

In this blog, I want to dive in a little deeper into this Riemannian manifold with the affine-invariant metric.

One more time, to recall the geometry of \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\)

Let

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \{ A \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} \mid A^\top = A,\; A > 0 \}.\]

This is a smooth manifold of dimension \(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\).

It can be identified with the symmetric space

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) \cong \mathrm{GL}(n)/\mathrm{O}(n).\]

Let’s start by figuring out where the affine-invariant metric comes from.

1] Deriving the Affine-Invariant Metric from First Principles

We want a Riemannian metric satisfying the following properties:

  1. Smoothness
  2. Positive definiteness
  3. Invariance under congruence transformations
\[A \mapsto M A M^\top \quad \text{for } M \in \mathrm{GL}(n).\]

This invariance is the key principle. Sidenote: this is different from similarity transformation (\(A \mapsto M A M^{-1} \quad \text{for } M \in \mathrm{GL}(n)\)).

Why do we need such invariance under congruence? Because SPD matrices represent quadratic forms.

If a quadratic form is \(q(x) = x^\top A x\) and we change coordinates \(y = Mx\), then \(q(y) = x^\top (M^\top A M) x\). Up to swapping left/right order, this is exactly congruence.

Consequently, congruence encodes the change of coordinates for quadratic forms. And we want the metric to be invariance under such coordinate changes.

On the other hand, it’s not hard to show that SPD stay SPD under congruence. Why? Let \(A \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\). Then for any nonzero vector \(x\),

\[x^\top (M A M^\top) x = (M^\top x)^\top A (M^\top x).\]

Since $M^\top x \neq 0$ whenever $x \neq 0$,

So:

\[M A M^\top \in \mathrm{SPD}(n).\]

Thus \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) is closed under congruence transformations.

Before we continue, it’s worth clarifying what “affine-invariant metric” mean. We say the metric is affine-invariant if:

\[d(A,B) = d(M A M^\top,\; M B M^\top), \forall M \in GL(n)\]

(should this be actually termed “congruence-invariant”? )

Meaning:

Distance does not depend on the coordinate system.

that means if we reparameterize space (this is the underlying vector space of \(x, y\), not the ambient space where \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) lives) by any invertible linear map, such defined distances between SPD matrices will remain unchanged. This is one key property we want to acquire.

Consequently, in some sense this is the “right” symmetry. Because SPD matrices encode:

  • Inner products
  • Covariance matrices
  • Metrics on vector spaces

Changing basis of the underlying vector space

\[v \mapsto M v\]

should not change intrinsic geometry. So the natural symmetry group acting on SPD is:

\[A \mapsto M A M^\top.\]

This also explains why we do not use similarity invariance. Notice that similarity preserves eigenvalues (not proved here), whereas congruence preserves:

  • Positive definiteness
  • Inertia (Sylvester’s law)

But eigenvalues change under congruence. In other words, congruence corresponds to changing coordinates in the domain, not changing the linear operator as a map. \(\mathrm{SPD}\) matrices are not being viewed as operators — they are being viewed as quadratic forms. This distinction is crucial.

Back to our goal. Now we are clear that we ideally require invariance under congruence. Notice that \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) is an open subset of symmetric matrices, so at any point \(A \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\),

\[T_A \mathrm{SPD}(n) = \mathrm{Sym}(n),\]

the space of symmetric matrices (I’ll leave it to the readers for the proof). So tangent vectors are symmetric matrices \(H,K\), and then our goal is to find \(g_A(H, K)\) that depends smoothly on \(A \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\).

Because of invariance, it suffices to define the metric at \(I\) (echoeing the definition of Lie derivative). Why? Any \(A \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\) can be sent to \(I\) by congruence of \(A^{-1/2}\). Thus, if we know the metric at \(I\), invariance determines it everywhere.

Then, we want the metric at identity to be invariant under the stabilizer group: \(I \rightarrow QIQ^\top = I\), where \(Q \in O(n)\), so the metric must satisfy

\[g_I(H,K) = g_I(QHQ^\top, QKQ^\top)\]

What bilinear forms on symmetric matrices have this invariance? By representation theory (or symmetry reasoning) the only such inner product (up to scaling) is:

\[g_I(H,K) = \mathrm{tr}(HK).\]

This is $\mathrm{O}(n)$-invariant (check it yourself) and there’s no other alternative.

Finally, to transport this inner product using congruence, given \(A \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\), transport back to \(I\) via:

\[H \mapsto A^{-1/2} H A^{-1/2}.\]

Consequently, we define:

\[g_A(H,K) = \mathrm{tr}\!\left( A^{-1/2} H A^{-1} K A^{-1/2} \right).\]

Consequently, the final metric is of the form

\[\boxed{ g_A(H,K) = \mathrm{tr}(A^{-1} H A^{-1} K) }\]

This is indeed the affine-invariant Riemannian metric we saw above.

To prove that it’s indeed a Riemannian metric, we need to prove that it varies smoothly over the input domain…

2] Distance and Geodesics

Using symmetry again, because the metric is affine-invariant, we can move \(A\) to identity without changing distance. Define \(C = A^{-1/2}BA^{-1/2}\), then

\[d(A, B) = d(I, C)\]

Consequently, we only need distance from identity, which we know specifically the metric, jus the Frobenius:

\[g_I(H, K) = tr(HK)\]

So near identity, the manifold looks Euclidean. Now consider the exponential curve (just matrix exponential for now, not apriori the exponential map per se):

\[\gamma(t) = \exp(tX)\]

which means

\[\dot{\gamma}(t) = \gamma(t)X\]

We then compute its speed:

\[||\dot{\gamma}(t)||^2_{\gamma(t)} = tr(\gamma(t)^{-1}\dot{\gamma}(t)\gamma(t)^{-1}\dot{\gamma}(t))\]

But since

\[\gamma^{-1}(t)\dot{\gamma}(t) = X\]

we obtain that

\[||\dot{\gamma}(t)||^2_{\gamma(t)} = tr(X^2)\]

which means the speed is constant, and that leads naturally to the fact that the exponential curves are geodesics.

From the above it’s easy to deduce the geodesic from \(I\) to any \(C \in \mathrm{SPD(n)}\): Write \(C = Q\Lambda Q^\top\), with eigenvalues \(\lambda_i > 0\). Then define \(X = Q diag(log(\lambda_i)) Q^\top\). Consequently \(C = exp(X)\). So the geodesic from identity to \(C\) is

\[\gamma(t) = exp(tX)\]

Because it’s constant speed from \(t = 0\) to \(t = 1\), the length of such geodesic is:

\[\sqrt{tr(X^2)}\]

Since \(X = Q diag(log(\lambda_i)) Q^\top\), we obtain

\[tr(X^2) = \sum_{1}^{n}log(\lambda_i)^2\]

thus

\[d(A, B)= \sqrt{\sum_{1}^{n}log(\lambda_i)^2}\]

where \(\lambda_i\) are eigenvalues of \(A^{-1}B\).

In short, what really happened here conceptually is that symmetry forced the metric, and the metric made the exponential map natural. Based on affine invariance, everything was reduced to identity at which the geometry is close to Euclidean. Logarithm linearizes multiplicative structure, so the geometry becomes Euclidean in log-eigenvalue coordinates.

[TODO]

There’s another way to look at the distance formula from above.

The natural next step is to calculate the geodesics given such Riemannian metric. Solving the geodesic equation gives:

\[\gamma(t) = A^{1/2} \left( A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2} \right)^t A^{1/2}.\]

Distance:

\[d(A,B) = \left\| \log(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}) \right\|_F.\]

If $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of $A^{-1}B$, then

\[d(A,B) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^n (\log \lambda_i)^2 \right)^{1/2}.\]

Please refer to this blog for deriving the distance formula based on eigenvalues.

3] Bigger Picture

At this point you might be wondering why exactly exponential curves solve the geodesic equation. There are two complementary ways to understand why exponential curves are geodesics on \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) under the affine-invariant metric:

  1. Lie–theoretic (symmetric space) argument, which is conceptual and clean
  2. Direct Riemannian computation, which is explicit and concrete

The first one is conceptual and clean, whereas the second one is explicit and concrete. Moreover, the first viewpoint belies a more fundamental fact. Let’s do both.

3.1] Symmetric space viewpoint

Recall:

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) \cong \mathrm{GL}(n)/\mathrm{O}(n).\]

This is a Riemannian symmetric space. [TODO]

Recall there is a general theorem:

On a Riemannian symmetric space \(G/K\), geodesics through the identity coset are exactly curves of the form \(\gamma(t) = \exp(tX) \cdot o,\) where \(X\) lies in the symmetric part of the Lie algebra.

For \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\):

  • Lie algebra of $\mathrm{GL}(n)$ = all matrices
  • Decomposition: \(\mathfrak{gl}(n) = \mathfrak{o}(n) \oplus \mathrm{Sym}(n)\) (skew + symmetric)

The tangent space of \(\mathrm{SPD(n)}\) at identity corresponds to symmetric matrices.

So geodesics through identity are:

\[\gamma(t) = \exp(tX), \quad X = X^\top.\]

This is a structural result of symmetric space geometry. So exponentials are not guessed — they are forced by the homogeneous structure.

3.2] Riemannian computation

Now let’s verify it directly. Notice that the affine-invariant metric is:

\[g_A(H,K) = \mathrm{tr}(A^{-1} H A^{-1} K).\]

We want curves $\gamma(t)$ minimizing energy:

\[E = \frac12 \int \mathrm{tr} \big( \gamma^{-1}(t) \dot{\gamma}(t) \gamma^{-1}(t) \dot{\gamma}(t) \big) \, dt.\]

Define the “velocity in body coordinates”:

\[V(t) = \gamma^{-1}(t)\dot{\gamma}(t).\]

Then the energy becomes:

\[E = \frac12 \int \mathrm{tr}(V^2) dt.\]

Now vary the curve. The Euler–Lagrange equation gives:

\[\dot{V} = 0.\]

So:

\[V(t) = X\]

for some constant symmetric matrix $X$.

But \(V = \gamma^{-1}(t) \dot{\gamma}(t)\), so we get:

\[\gamma^{-1}(t) \dot{\gamma}(t) = X.\]

Multiply both sides by \(\gamma (t)\):

\[\dot{\gamma}(t) = \gamma(t) X.\]

This is a matrix differential equation.

Solve it:

\[\gamma(t) = \gamma(0)\exp(tX).\]

That’s it. So exponential curves solve the geodesic equation because:

The metric makes the energy depend only on $\gamma^{-1}\dot{\gamma}$, and minimizing energy forces that quantity to be constant.

3.3] Why does this happen geometrically?

This is the key geometric reason: the metric is left-invariant under congruence action. When a metric has strong symmetry:

  • Geodesics correspond to 1-parameter subgroups
  • Motion with constant “Lie algebra velocity” is straight

Here the straight line condition is:

\[\gamma^{-1}\dot{\gamma} = \text{constant}.\]

This is the nonlinear analogue of:

\[\dot{x} = \text{constant}\]

in Euclidean space.

3.4] Why symmetric \(X\)?

Because:

  • Tangent space at identity = symmetric matrices
  • If \(X\) were skew-symmetric, \(\exp(tX)\) would stay in \(\mathrm{O}(n)\)
  • Symmetric \(X\) keeps us inside SPD

Thus:

\[X = X^\top.\]

3.5] Intuition in log coordinates

Consider the map:

\[A \mapsto \log A.\]

Near identity:

\[\gamma(t) = \exp(tX)\]

becomes:

\[\log \gamma(t) = tX.\]

So in log-coordinates:

Geodesics are straight lines.

This is the cleanest intuition.

3.6] Big picture summary

Exponential curves solve the geodesic equation because:

  1. SPD is a symmetric space.
  2. The metric depends only on \(\gamma^{-1}\dot{\gamma}\).
  3. Minimizing energy forces that quantity to be constant.
  4. Constant Lie algebra velocity integrates to exponentials.
  5. Logarithm linearizes the geometry.

So exponentials are not magic — they are the nonlinear version of straight lines.

4] Why Curvature Is Negative

Recall:

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) \cong \mathrm{GL}(n)/\mathrm{O}(n).\]

This is indeed a noncompact Riemannian symmetric space.

Notice that we have a general theorem:

If \(G/K\) is noncompact semisimple, then sectional curvature is nonpositive.

Since $\mathrm{GL}(n)$ is noncompact and $\mathrm{O}(n)$ is compact,

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n)\]

has nonpositive sectional curvature.

The intuition for the above is that if we investigate using the logarithm map:

\[A \mapsto \log A.\]

Eigenvalues become:

\[\lambda_i \mapsto \log \lambda_i.\]

Distance becomes Euclidean in log-eigenvalue coordinates:

\[d^2 = \sum (\log \lambda_i)^2.\]

Logarithm turns multiplicative changes into additive ones. Consequently, multiplicative geometry is turned into hyperbolic-like geometry which entails negative curvature.

It’s also true that in this case geodesics spread apart faster than Euclidean ones.

3] Exponential and Logarithmic Map

4] Connection to Wasserstein Distance

Consider zero-mean Gaussian measures:

\[\mathcal{N}(0,A), \quad \mathcal{N}(0,B).\]

Their 2-Wasserstein distance is:

\[W_2^2(A,B) = \mathrm{tr}(A) + \mathrm{tr}(B) - 2\,\mathrm{tr} \left( (A^{1/2} B A^{1/2})^{1/2} \right).\]

This induces the Bures metric on SPD.

If we do a direct comparison

Affine-invariant metric:

  • Arises from group symmetry
  • Negative curvature
  • Multiplicative geodesics

Wasserstein/Bures metric:

  • Arises from optimal transport
  • Related to quantum information
  • Different curvature structure

Both treat SPD matrices as a curved manifold, but from different geometric principles.

5] Connection to Fisher Information

Consider the family of Gaussians:

\[p(x;A) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \det(A)^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac12 x^\top A^{-1} x \right).\]

If we compute the Fisher information metric:

\[g_A(H,K) = \mathbb{E} \left[ \partial_H \log p \; \partial_K \log p \right].\]

One obtains:

\[\boxed{ g_A(H,K) = \frac12 \mathrm{tr}(A^{-1} H A^{-1} K) }\]

Up to a constant factor. Indeed, this is exactly the affine-invariant metric.

Consequently, this offers another interpretation of the affine-invariant metric as

  • The Fisher information metric
  • The natural gradient metric
  • The intrinsic geometry of Gaussian covariance matrices

Thus:

SPD geometry = Information geometry of Gaussians.

6] Summary

\(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) simultaneously appears as:

  1. A symmetric space: \(\mathrm{GL}(n)/\mathrm{O}(n)\)

  2. A negatively curved manifold under affine-invariant metric

  3. The covariance manifold of Gaussians under Fisher metric

  4. The state space of quantum density matrices under Bures metric

This is why it appears in optimal transport, information geometry, machine learning, and etc.

entry.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (X): Riemannian metric and Riemannian manifold (in progress)

link.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (X): Riemannian metric and Riemannian manifold (in progress)

entry.content:
0] Intuition and Introduction

1] Riemannian Metric

2] Riemannian Manifold

2.1] Definitions

2.2] Examples

2.2.1] Symmetric Positive Definite Matrix

Recall from the blog on Lie group/Lie algebra that we talked about the set of symmetric positive definite matrices:

\(\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \{ A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \mid A^\top = A,\; A > 0 \}.\),

which is not actually a Lie group but a smooth manifold. In particular, this framework pops up a lot in brian signals analysis given that data (spatial) covariance matrices are exactly \(\mathrm{SPD}\) matrices (reference …).

Since \(\mathrm{SPD}(n)\) is a manifold, then we could naturally apply some Riemannian metric to it, after which we could calculate distances and geodesics. Of course, there is no unique way of specifying a metric and there are several important ones. To have a taste of how that might look like, let me present the observations in a backward order: Based on different selected metrics, we might have different notions of distances as below:

  • (A) Euclidean / Frobenius distance
\[d(A,B) = \|A - B\|_F.\]

Here we just treat matrices and vectors. This is simple but not geometrically natural, and it’s not invariant under coordinate changes. Note that to make this Euclidean, we actually applied the Frobenius inner product \(<A, B>_X = Tr(A^\top B)\).

  • (B) Affine-Invariant Riemannian Metric (AIRM, Canonical)
\[d(A,B) = \left\| \log\left(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}\right) \right\|_F.\]

This distance has specific properties:

  • Geometrically natural
  • Invariant under congruence transforms: \(A \mapsto M A M^\top\)
  • Nonpositive curvature
  • Geodesic: \(\gamma(t) = A^{1/2} \left(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}\right)^t A^{1/2}.\) (which is nicely multiplicative)

Notice that this metric turns \(\mathrm{SPD(n)}\) into a Riemannian symmetric space. The affine-invariant metric is the canonical one because it respects the natural symmetry of the space.

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \mathrm{GL}(n) / \mathrm{O}(n).\]
  • (C) Log-Euclidean Metric
\[d(A,B) = \|\log A - \log B\|_F.\]

Here log refers to matrix logarithm. This computation is very easy, and makes the space “flat” after log transform.

  • (D) Bures–Wasserstein Metric
\[d^2(A,B) = \mathrm{tr}(A) + \mathrm{tr}(B) - 2 \mathrm{tr}\left( (A^{1/2} B A^{1/2})^{1/2} \right).\]

This choice is deeply related to optimal transport and quantum information theory.

I want to dwell here a little longer to illustrate the computation of the affine-invariant distance. Practically, the process is as follows:

Let \(A, B \in \mathrm{SPD}(n)\).

The affine-invariant distance, as presented above, is

\[d(A,B) = \left\| \log\left(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}\right) \right\|_F.\]

We first do diagonalization: since \(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}\) is SPD,

\[A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2} = Q \Lambda Q^\top,\]

where

\[\Lambda = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n), \quad \lambda_i > 0.\]

These $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of \(A^{-1}B\) (they are also called the generalized eigenvalues of the pair \((A,B)\)). Then we apply matrix logarithm and it’s not hard to see that

\[\log(A^{-1/2} B A^{-1/2}) = Q \mathrm{diag}(\log \lambda_1,\dots,\log \lambda_n) Q^\top.\]

On the other hand, notice that the Frobenius norm is invariant under orthogonal conjugation:

\[\|Q D Q^\top\|_F = \|D\|_F.\]

So

\[d(A,B) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^n (\log \lambda_i)^2 \right)^{1/2}.\]

This final formula is very clean: if \(\lambda_i\) are eigenvalues of \(A^{-1}B\), then:

\[\boxed{ d(A,B) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^n (\log \lambda_i)^2 \right)^{1/2} }\]

Furthermore, to connect back to the log-Euclidean metric, notice that one special case: if \(A\) and \(B\) commute (so they would share eigenvalues):

\[d(A,B) = \|\log A - \log B\|_F.\]

We could interpret this distance as

  • Diagonalize in coordinates where \(A = I\).
  • Measure how much \(B\) stretches each principal axis.
  • Take the Euclidean norm of the log-stretches.

So the distance is just Euclidean distance between the log-eigenvalues.

The affine-invariant metric makes SPD matrices behave like \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (in log-eigenvalue coordinates), except that

  • eigenvectors rotate smoothly,
  • curvature is nonpositive.
entry.summary: Exploration of metric, and Riemannian manifold

entry.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (5): Lie Group and Lie Algebra (in progress)

link.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (5): Lie Group and Lie Algebra (in progress)

entry.content:
0] Introduction and Intuition

1] Lie Group

2] Lie Algebra

3] Computation and Examples

Matrix groups and computations.

3.1] Symmetric Matrix

Visualization/reason of why it is not a Lie group?

3.2] Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) Matrix

3.2.1] Definition and Geometric Intuition

Let’s start with the real case. A real matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes n}\) is called positive definite if

\[x^ op A x > 0 \quad ext{for all } x eq 0.\]

Perhaps a necessary clarification here: a positive definite matrix is NOT necessarily symmetric. What actually happens is, say we write \(A\) as

\[A = S + K\]

where

\[S = rac{A + A^ op}{2}, \qquad K = rac{A - A^ op}{2}.\]

Here:

  • \(S\) is symmetric
  • $K$ is skew-symmetric

Now compute, simply by linearity:

\[x^ op A x = x^ op S x + x^ op K x.\]

Notice that for any skew-symmetric matrix \(K\),

\[x^ op K x = 0.\]

Therefore,

\[x^ op A x = x^ op S x.\]

This means that the quadratic form only “sees” the symmetric part. Consequently, if \(x^ op A x > 0\) for all \(x eq 0\), then the symmetric part \(S\) is positive definite. Hence, positive definiteness does NOT force \(A\) to be symmetric. It only forces the symmetric part of \(A\) to be positive definite. On the other hand, we can add any skew-symmetric matrix to a symmetric positive definite matrix and the quadratic form will not change at all. \(A\) can definitely be non-symmetric.

It’s also true that because the value of the “test” depends entirely on the symmetric part, many mathematicians find it cleaner to simply define positive definiteness for symmetric matrices only.

For many textbooks it’s typically required that a PD matrix to be also symmetric. That’s because it has very strong structure, like real positive eigenvalues, orthogonal diagonalization, and it thus defines an inner product and a Riemannian metric (we will come back to this later on), etc. Without symmetry, eigenvalues can be complex, matrix may not be diagonalizable, and the spectral theory breaks. So in analysis and geometry, “positive definite matrix” is usually defined to mean symmetric positive definite. That’s sometimes also a convention.

The geometric intuition for the fact of skew-symmetric matrix adding no effects on the quadratic form is that skew-symmetric part represents an infinitesimal rotation. And rotations do not change \(x^ op Ax\) because quadratic forms measure “energy” or “stretching”, and rotations don’t stretch — they just spin. \(S\) controls stretching, and \(K\) controls rotation — the quadratic form only detects stretching.

Another definition of positive definite is to require

    1. \(A\) symmetric,
    1. All eigenvalues are positive

where symmetry is explicitly added as part of the definition (just by condition 2 we do NOT necessarily obtain \(x^ op Ax \geq 0\)).

So in practice, people define:

\[\mathrm{Sym}(n) = \{ A \mid A^ op = A, A \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes n} \}\]

and then set up the following

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \{ A \in \mathrm{Sym}(n) \mid A > 0 \}.\]

Thus SPD means Symmetric Positive Definite, where the “S” is mostly for clarity and convention.

Finally, to complicate/complete the story a little bit more, for complex matrices, for \(z^* M z\) to be real and positive, \(M\) must be Hermitian (the complex analogue of symmetry, I’ll not expand here).

3.2.2] Geometry of SPD Matrices

Let

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \{ A \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes n} \mid A^ op = A,\; A > 0 \}.\]

First of all, is \(\mathrm{SPD}\) a group?

The answer is no. Why? Because

  • Not closed under addition (no additive inverse inside)
  • Not closed under multiplication (product not symmetric: if \(A, B \in SPD(n)\), then in general \(AB otin SPD(n)\)). Consequently, it’s obvious that the space of symmetric positive definite (\(\mathrm{SPD}\)) matrices is not a Lie group under multiplication.

However, notice that \(GL(n)\) is a Lie group, and

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n) = \mathrm{GL}(n) / \mathrm{O}(n).\]

Consequently, although it’s not a Lie group, it is indeed

  • A smooth manifold of dimension \( rac{n(n+1)}{2}\)
  • A Riemannian symmetric space

3.2.3] What topology?

Given that we claim it’s a manifold, you may wonder what topology we are using here. Specifically, we identify symmetric matrices with vectors \(\mathbb{R}^{n(n+1)/2}\) in the Euclidean space. The topology is simply the subspace topology inherited from Euclidean space.

Why then is it a manifold? Because symmetric matrices form a vector space, positive definiteness is an open condition, and eigenvalues depend continuously on entries.

In effect, \(\mathrm{SPD}\) matrices form an open cone.

Therefore:

\[\mathrm{SPD}(n)\]

is an open convex cone in \(\mathrm{Sym(n)}\), the vector space of symmetric matrices.
entry.summary: Lie group, Lie algebra, and their computation
